title: Xanthoxalis dumetorum
intro: Xanthoxalis dumetorum, also known as sheep's sorrel, is a perennial plant species belonging to the buckwheat family (Polygonaceae). This plant has been traditionally used for its potential health benefits, including its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions such as diarrhea, fever, and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Xanthoxalis dumetorum include ascorbic acid (vitamin C), anthraquinones, and other flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Xanthoxalis dumetorum include teas, infusions, and salves, which are often used to support immune function and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Xanthoxalis dumetorum is a species of flowering plant in the family Oxalidaceae. It is native to South America and is a member of the genus Xanthoxalis. The species is often classified under the synonym Oxalis dillenii. This plant is part of the larger group of flowering plants known as angiosperms. The classification of Xanthoxalis dumetorum is also reflected in its place within the broader plant kingdom, kingdom Plantae.

side_effects: Xanthoxalis dumetorum, also known as woodland sorrel, is not commonly reported to have severe side effects when consumed in moderation. However, eating large quantities of the plant can cause gastrointestinal issues, including nausea and diarrhea, due to its high oxalic acid content. Some individuals may also experience skin irritation or allergic reactions after coming into contact with the plant's sap or leaves. Xanthoxalis dumetorum should be avoided by pregnant women and those with kidney or liver problems, as its toxicity could exacerbate these conditions. Ingesting the plant in large amounts can also lead to kidney damage over time.
